text: The video shows an inflammatory mass in the right lower quadrant covered by omentum, with a visible intero-intero fistula. The surgeon identifies a plane between the two small bowel mesenteries, preparing to use a stapler to transect the fistula. The inflammatory mass is located high in the mesentery, while the base appears normal.

The surgeon scores the peritoneum over the ileocolic vessel with traction applied, allowing placement of a 10mm bipolar instrument directly over the vessel. A medial to lateral dissection is performed, developing the proper plane and encompassing the ileocolic vessel with the duodenum beneath it. The bipolar device is used to divide the vessels, and the traction on the mesentery is released to minimize bleeding. The vessel is visibly transected.

The assistant uses a grasper to elevate the colon mesentery, providing exposure to the retroperitoneum for a medial lateral dissection. The surgeon divides the peritoneum over the mesentery to reach the colon wall, using a ten millimeter bipolar device to divide vessels in the proper plane beneath the colon. The dissection progresses across normal bowel, where a sixty millimeter stapler is used to transect the bowel in one firing.

The surgeon activates and fires the stapler to transect the bowel. Following this, the ileal dissection is performed by dividing the peritoneum adjacent to the bowel wall. The ileum appears slightly dilated due to obstructive disease. Scissors are passed beneath the ileum, creating an opening for the stapler to pass through, allowing for the division of the ileum in normal bowel to pedicalize the ileocolic specimen for removal.

The surgeon uses traction and monopolar energy to approach the inflammatory mass from the lateral side, following the previously completed medial dissection. The lateral dissection of the cecal appendiceal ascending colon is performed using a ten millimeter bipolar instrument, revealing the proximity and ease of accessing the dissection site.

The surgeon elevates both sides of the ileal mesentery, scoring it with a bipolar device while staying close to the bowel intended for removal. The peritoneum over the mesentery is divided to facilitate direct application of the energy device to the vessel, ensuring the blood supply to the remaining bowel is preserved. The fragile nature of the ileal mesentery in Crohn's disease is evident, as gentle manipulation causes bleeding.

The surgeon completes the division of the mesentery, fully freeing the specimen, which is stored in the pelvis for later extraction. The in situ ileocolic mesentery base is visible, showing good blood supply. The surgeon prepares for an intracorporeal anastomosis, ensuring the mesentery and bowel are aligned to prevent twisting during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a hook electrode with cutting current to create an enterotomy in the bowel lumen, ensuring no false passage is made. The same instrument is used to make an enterotomy in the colon. The proximal small bowel is placed onto the jaw of a 60mm stapler, followed by the colon side. Using grasping forceps, the ends of the bowel are aligned at the stapler jaws, which are then closed and fired. The staple line is elevated to prevent spillage of bowel contents, and bleeding is controlled using a bipolar device or sutures.

The surgeon uses a two-ovicral suture to close the enterotomy past the staple line, tying the knot intracorporeally with the knot positioned towards themselves and the tail away, ensuring the loop remains secure during the tying process.

The surgeon uses an assistance device to elevate the bowel, positioning it at a ninety-degree angle for suturing. A full-thickness suture is applied to close the enterotomy, with the needle being pulled and placed precisely using the tips of the instruments. The corner of the suture is tucked in using a small five-millimeter instrument, and the suture is tightened and tied.

The surgeon performs a two-layer closure of the enterotomy using a 3-0 Prolene suture, making the loop towards themselves and the tail away. The suture material slides easily, allowing placement of three sutures at once before pulling them through. The surgeon uses the tips of instruments to control the needles and threads, passing serial muscular stitches between the original stitches.

The surgeon makes three throws of the suture without pulling them through initially, demonstrating how the suture material slides easily through the bowel wall. Debris is removed from the surgical field, and the suture is carried to the end and tied, completing the anastomosis. The anastomosis is visibly isoparastaltic with good blood supply.
